1: hallĕlû-yāh šîrû layhwh šîr ḥādāš tĕhillātô biqhal ḥăsîdîm — "Praise the LORD! Sing to the LORD a new song; his praise in the assembly of the faithful." šîr ḥādāš (new song — the phrase appears at Ps 33:3; 40:3; 96:1; 98:1; 144:9; Isa 42:10; Rev 5:9; 14:3; always in response to a new or decisive act of divine salvation). The newness is not aesthetic novelty but theological freshness: each new divine act of redemption requires a new praise-response. biqhal ḥăsîdîm (in the assembly of the faithful/devoted — from qāhal, the assembled community of Israel, and ḥăsîdîm, those characterized by ḥesed, covenant loyalty). The ḥăsîdîm are this psalm's central subject — they appear in vv 1, 5, and 9, forming an inclusio.
2: yiśmaḥ yiśrāʾēl bĕʿōśāyw bĕnê-ṣiyyôn yāgîlû bĕmalkām — "let Israel rejoice in their Maker; let the people of Zion be joyful in their King." ʿōśāyw (their Maker — Qal active participle of ʿāśāh, to make/do; plural of majesty construction — not "their makers" but the one Maker described by a plural of excellence). yiśmaḥ (rejoice — Qal jussive of śāmaḥ) + yāgîlû (be joyful — Qal jussive of gîl, to exult, spin with joy; a more exuberant word than śāmaḥ). malkām (their King) — YHWH as the divine King of Zion; the title connects the rejoicing community to the royal enthronement psalms (Ps 93; 95–99).
3: yĕhallĕlû šĕmô bĕmaḥôl bĕtōp wĕkinnôr yĕzammĕrû-lô — "let them praise his name with dancing; let them make music to him with tambourine and lyre." māḥôl (dance — the round dance, celebratory movement; used of Miriam's dance in Exod 15:20 and David's before the ark in 2 Sam 6:14). tōp (tambourine/hand-drum — the instrument Miriam took up after the Reed Sea, Exod 15:20) + kinnôr (lyre/harp — David's instrument). The echo of the Exodus praise-instruments situates Ps 149's praise within the tradition of Israel's great victory celebrations. The worship is fully embodied: voice (praise), movement (dance), and multiple instruments.
4: kî-rōṣeh yhwh bĕʿammô yĕpāʾēr ʿănāwîm bîšûʿāh — "for the LORD takes pleasure in his people; he adorns the humble with salvation." rāṣāh (takes pleasure/delight in — Qal imperfect; the same verb as Ps 147:11 where YHWH takes pleasure in those who fear him and hope in his ḥesed; here the object is simply ʿammô, his people). yĕpāʾēr (adorns/beautifies/glorifies — Piel imperfect of pāʾar, to beautify, adorn, glorify; the Piel of intensive beautification) the ʿănāwîm (humble/poor/afflicted — the same class as Ps 147:6) with yĕšûʿāh (salvation, victory — the root of the name Jesus/Yĕšûaʿ). Salvation as an adornment: the humble are clothed in divine victory, which functions as their honor-garment in a society where honor was everything.
5: yĕʿallzû ḥăsîdîm bĕkābôd yĕrannĕnû ʿal-miškĕbôtām — "let the faithful exult in glory; let them sing aloud even on their beds." ʿālaz (exult — Qal imperfect of ʿālaz, to rejoice, be jubilant; a strong emotional word for exuberant joy). bĕkābôd (in glory — either YHWH's glory as the ground of their exultation, or the glory that accrues to the ḥăsîdîm through the divine vindication of v4). yĕrannĕnû (sing aloud with joy — Piel imperfect of rānan, to ring out in joy, to shout aloud; the root of rinnāh, the ringing cry of praise) ʿal-miškĕbôtām (on their beds) — even in sleep-settings, the praise continues; possibly the night-vigil of those who meditate on YHWH in the night watches (Ps 63:6; 119:55). The midnight praise is as genuine as the daylight assembly.
6: rômmôt ʾēl bigĕrônām wĕḥereb pîpiyyôt bĕyādām — "with the high praises of God in their throats and a double-edged sword in their hands." The psalm's most striking image: two paired instruments of the ḥăsîdîm. rômmôt ʾēl (high praises of God — plural of rômāh, high things, exaltations; from rûm, to be high; these are praises that exalt God to his proper height). bigĕrônām (in their throats/gullets — from gārôn, the throat as the channel of vocal sound; Ps 115:7 uses it for throats that do not speak). ḥereb pîpiyyôt (double-edged sword — literally "sword of mouths"; peh = mouth; a sword with two mouths/edges, both cutting). The same image appears in Rev 19:15 (the rider's sword from his mouth) and Heb 4:12 (the word of God as a two-edged sword). The ḥăsîdîm are simultaneously worshippers and warriors — their praise and their action both serve YHWH's purposes.
7: laʿăśôt nĕqāmāh baggôyim tôkĕḥôt bĕlĕʾummîm — "to execute vengeance on the nations and punishment on the peoples." nĕqāmāh (vengeance — from nāqam, to avenge; YHWH's vengeance is judicial rather than personal; the divine warrior's act of setting right what was wronged). The ḥăsîdîm act as agents of this divine judicial action — not as initiators of personal revenge but as instruments through whom YHWH executes judgment. tôkĕḥôt (punishments/corrections — from yākaḥ, to reprove, correct, decide; the judicial sense of warranted correction, not arbitrary violence). The scope — nations and peoples — suggests an eschatological or historical judgment rather than an individual grievance.
8: lĕʾsōr malkêhem bĕziqîm wĕnikabbĕdêhem bĕkablê barzel — "to bind their kings in chains and their nobles in iron fetters." ʾāsar (bind — Qal infinitive construct with lĕ; the binding of captives as the completion of military victory, Josh 10:24; Judg 1:7). ziqîm (chains — restraints for prisoners) + kablê barzel (iron fetters — from kebesel, fetter; the iron chains of serious imprisonment, Ps 107:10, 14). nikabbĕdêhem (their nobles/honored ones — Niphal participle of kābad, to be heavy/honored; those who have social weight and dignity). The binding of kings and nobles in iron chains is the imagery of complete military conquest; the enemies who exalted themselves are brought low.
9: laʿăśôt bāhem mišpāṭ kātûb hādār hûʾ lĕkol-ḥăsîdāyw hallĕlû-yāh — "to execute on them the judgment that is written — this is the honor/glory of all his faithful ones. Praise the LORD!" mišpāṭ kātûb (the judgment that is written — the decree already recorded in Scripture; most naturally Deut 28–32, the covenant curses that would come upon the nations that oppose YHWH's people, or more broadly the Torah's prophetic vision of divine justice). hādār hûʾ lĕkol-ḥăsîdāyw (this is the honor/splendor for all his faithful ones — hādār, the majestic honor/beauty, same word as YHWH's hôd wĕhādār in Ps 104:1; 111:3). The privilege of executing YHWH's written judgment is itself the hādār of the ḥăsîdîm — their dignity comes from participating in divine justice, not from inherent status. hallĕlû-yāh — the doxological close that mirrors v1's opening.
